claims.claim_text: Codebase structure also matters: AI tools perform worse against shallow, leaky abstractions because they force the model to reason across too many layers simultaneously, while [deep modules with narrow interfaces](/wiki/api-design) keep reasoning tractable [deep modules](/reading/2026-05/2026-05-04t231343-ai-likes-deep-modules).
claims.justification: The source states that 'shallow, leaky abstractions force LLMs to reason across too many layers at once' and that 'AI coding tools work best with codebases built around deep modules — interfaces that hide complexity,' which directly entails the claim.

claims.claim_text: Multi-agent systems research finds that modality shift, checking output in a different representation than it was produced in, separates meaningful structural gates from weak self-evaluation [verification patterns](/reading/2026-05/2026-05-03t110102-getting-up-to-speed-on-multi-agent-systems-part-6).
claims.justification: The source summary states that 'modality shift -- checking work in a different representation than it was produced in -- is the key variable that separates weak self-verification from strong structural gates,' which directly entails the claim.

claims.claim_text: Benchmarks used to evaluate these systems were mostly designed for single agents and fail to measure [coordination overhead or failure recovery](/wiki/agent-coordination), making published results from frameworks like ChatDev and MetaGPT difficult to compare [benchmarks](/reading/2026-05/2026-05-03t110114-getting-up-to-speed-on-multi-agent-systems-part-7).
claims.justification: The source states 'Most MAS benchmarks were designed for single agents and can't measure coordination quality, communication overhead, or failure recovery' and explicitly names ChatDev and MetaGPT as examples of papers reporting contradictory results, directly supporting the claim.
